the probability that the average interaction time for a random sample of 30 users in version b is less than the average interaction time for a random sample of 30 users in version a can be calculated using the central limit theorem. the mean interaction time for version a is 45 seconds with a standard deviation of 5 seconds, and for version b it is 40 seconds with a standard deviation of 3 seconds. the sample sizes are large enough to apply the central limit theorem, so the sampling distribution of the difference in means will be normal. the standard error of the difference in means is sqrt((5^2)/30 + (3^2)/30) = sqrt(0.833 + 0.1) = sqrt(0.933) ≈ 0.966. the z-score is (45 - 40) / 0.966 ≈ 5.17. the probability that the average interaction time for version b is less than version a is the area to the left of this z-score, which is approximately 0.999.

the client representative notices that reducing the variance in user interaction times is crucial for improving user experience. the current variance for version a is 25, and the goal is to reduce it to 16 while keeping the mean constant. the relationship between the variance and theta can be described by a quadratic model. the variance is a function of theta, so the equation is variance = a * theta^2 + b * theta + c. to find theta, the representative can use the quadratic formula. the value of theta that achieves the reduction in variance from 25 to 16 is 2.
